data.title: EMTAR Demonstrates Intelligent Wireless System for 6G Non-Terrestrial Networks
data.description:
EMTAR Demonstrates Intelligent Wireless System for 6G Non-Terrestrial Networks

EMTAR Technologies today announced the successful silicon validation of its latest chip for 6G non-terrestrial networks (NTN). Developed in collaboration with TSMC as a direct account, the new platform enables highly integrated and intelligent connectivity solutions on a single chip, delivering exceptional sensitivity, energy efficiency, and ultra-low latency signal processing—key enablers for next-generation sensing and communication.

With this silicon validation, EMTAR has demonstrated proven performance, offering significant advantages in power efficiency, signal integrity, and wireless connectivity. The highly integrated system architecture incorporates advanced RF/analog and mixed-signal design techniques, delivering ultra-low noise, wide output power dynamic range, extremely low power consumption, and rapid response through built-in mixed-signal processing for real-time adaptability.

EMTAR’s intelligent system architecture enables consistent sensitivity, ultra-low latency, and stable throughput across diverse and dynamic channel conditions, fully aligned with next-generation NTN performance standards. The system achieves substantial improvements in power efficiency, extended wireless range, and signal responsiveness. These performance gains open the door to a new generation of compact, lightweight devices and terminals, making deployment feasible in environments limited by thermal constraints, latency demands, or strict link budgets.

Engineered with system-level innovation and focused on deployment practicality, EMTAR’s platform addresses long-standing end-user challenges without compromising size, weight, or power. It is particularly suited for 6G use cases, including drones, connected vehicles, and high-altitude platforms (HAPs), where mobility, reliability, and scalable connectivity are critical. The solution also supports integration with secure cloud infrastructure. With end-to-end data sovereignty and security, including encrypted intelligence and private network support, EMTAR empowers clients to maintain full control over their data while achieving scalable, secure, and high-performance connectivity.

“The successful silicon validation of EMTAR’s latest chip for 6G non-terrestrial networks marks a milestone in bringing our intelligent wireless system chip to market,” said Alvis Huang, Founder & CEO of EMTAR Technologies. “As a direct account with TSMC, EMTAR has the flexibility and depth of collaboration needed to deliver high-performance solutions that meet the stringent requirements of emerging 6G NTN applications. This milestone highlights our commitment to innovation that solves real customer challenges and supports the rapid growth of next-generation wireless markets.”

About EMTAR Technologies Inc.

Founded in 2023, EMTAR Technologies is a fabless semiconductor company pioneering next-generation wireless System-on-Chips (SoCs) for 6G non-terrestrial networks (NTN). EMTAR designs ultra-reliable, low-latency, and power-efficient wireless SoCs for mobile edge, infrastructure, and aerospace applications. The company is rapidly expanding its R&D capabilities and ecosystem partnerships to accelerate the global adoption of 6G NTN technologies.

For more information, visit our website or follow us on LinkedIn.

For media inquiries, partnership opportunities, or more information on our groundbreaking technologies, please contact us at info@emtartech.com.

Media Contact
Louisa Chen, info@emtartech.com

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251024420492/en/

data.title: CMG Financial Named a Top Employer for Women for the Fourth Consecutive Year by Mortgage Women Magazine
data.description:
SAN RAMON, CALIFORNIA / ACCESS Newswire / October 24, 2025 / CMG Financial, has once again been recognized as a Top Employer for Women by Mortgage Women Magazine, marking the fourth consecutive year the company has earned this distinction. This continued recognition underscores CMG's commitment to empowering women at every level of the organization and across the mortgage industry.

"At CMG, women are not just part of the conversation, they're leading it," said Melissa Harbourne, Chief of Staff at CMG. "We've built a company where women have the resources, flexibility, and opportunities to thrive - whether they're starting out in the industry or leading at the executive level."

CMG's culture of inclusivity and advancement has established it as a standout employer in the mortgage space. Women drive innovation and performance across the company, representing over 73% of CMG's operations teams and holding leadership positions at every level - from managers to executives. In 2023, CMG proudly had the most women recognized in National Mortgage Professional's Powerful Women of Mortgage Banking awards, a testament to the strength and influence of its female leaders. The company offers paid maternity leave, equal pay initiatives, and extensive professional development opportunities.

With women serving as the backbone of its success, CMG remains committed to building a workplace where talent, leadership, and innovation know no gender boundaries.

Read more here.

About CMG Financial
CMG Financial is a well-capitalized mortgage lender founded in 1993. Founder and CEO, Christopher M. George, was Chairman of the Mortgage Bankers Association in 2019. CMG makes its products and services available to the market through three distinct origination channels including retail lending, wholesale lending, and correspondent lending. CMG currently operates in all states, including the District of Columbia, and holds approvals with FNMA, FHLMC, and GNMA. CMG is widely known throughout the mortgage banking and housing markets for responsible lending practices, industry and consumer advocacy, product innovation, and operational efficiency. CMG Mortgage, Inc. NMLS ID# 1820 (www.nmlsconsumeraccess.org http://www.cmghomeloans.com).

Contact Information

Annaugh Madsen
Senior Copywriter
amadsen@cmgfi.com
(667) 260-6360

data.title: Why 10-year Treasury yield may hit 6% in next year or two on problematic inflation
data.description:
By Vivien Lou Chen

Once banks become incentivized to boost loans, household and business spending will likely support higher inflation, said economist Steven Blitz of GlobalData TS Lombard

Wall Street investors cheered Friday's September consumer-price data, but may not be out of the woods on inflation after this year.

U.S. stocks jumped Friday after September's consumer-price index came in below expectations, helping to support the case for Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts next week and in December.

Yet there are reasons to be cautious about where inflation may be headed beyond the next handful of months.

According to Steven Blitz, managing director and chief U.S. economist at GlobalData TS Lombard, inflation can still become "problematic" because of how it may work its way through the banking system and into the economy. And in his view, this is one factor that could push the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield BX:TMUBMUSD10Y up to 6% or even higher in the next year or two, from Friday's level of close to 4%.

On Friday, all three major U.S. stock indexes DJIA SPX COMP touched record intraday highs after September's CPI report showed inflation had eased by more than economists had expected.

Eric Sterner, chief investment officer at Apollon Wealth Management, which manages roughly $12 billion from Mount Pleasant, S.C., said "we are seeing disinflationary trends within many components of inflation, including shelter." Still, he acknowledges that "inflation is going to be sticky and it will take a while for the Fed to get back to its 2% inflation target."

Meanwhile, Treasury yields were little changed to slightly lower, with the 3-year yield BX:TMUBMUSD03Y falling to 3.48% versus an overnight fed-funds rate that was around 4.11%. Fed-funds futures were pricing in a decent chance of two or three additional quarter-point rate cuts between next January and next June, which would lead to a total of four or five cuts from now through mid-2026.

In the short term, "the economy is weaker than the market thinks, inflation is going to come off and, therefore, the Fed will be cutting rates," Blitz said via phone on Friday. "Will it be four or five times? Probably not." He added that his views on the path of inflation are centered on a time period beyond this year.

By his way of thinking, banks will be incentivized to unleash a surge in lending once the 3-year Treasury yield begins to rise above the shorter overnight rate, producing a positive or upwardly sloping yield curve between the two rates. Such a development would theoretically occur when there is a switch in the way market participants are thinking about U.S. growth, inflation and overall macro trends, which might happen next summer or even sooner, he said.

If market participants' expectations switch toward the possibility of Fed interest-rate hikes and away from the current consensus view of rate cuts, this would likely cause the short-term yield curve to steepen and bank lending to start growing, according to Blitz.

The slope of the short end of the Treasury yield curve matters to banks in terms of how aggressive they are in building their loan books, according to Blitz. A negative curve holds down loan growth, while a positive curve does the opposite: It supports increased lending because it gives banks room to shift their reserve assets into loans.

"Bank lending begins in earnest once that short curve turns positive, and we could begin to see that sooner than next summer," Blitz said via phone. "I wouldn't be shocked to see that in the second quarter, but it would require a positive [short-term] curve and positive outlook on growth."

Increased bank lending "is going to put an inflationary bias into the economy" by boosting the spending of businesses and households, which in turn will help areas like the housing industry, he said. In addition, persistent federal deficits, growth in government spending and disruptions in trade that "do not allow the economy to benefit from imported low prices" could all add up to a 10-year yield that starts to rise sharply from current levels over a period of time.

While a surge in bank lending from a positive short curve would not happen in one week, it "illustrates the inflationary power waiting to be released once the curve, Federal spending and tax incentives are all aligned in the same direction," Blitz wrote in a note on Friday, before the release of the September CPI data. Meanwhile, "the longer trend is up in yield, to a 6% 10-year and perhaps higher. Not in the next six months but in the next year or two."

Blitz isn't alone in his concerns about the likely path of inflation beyond 2025.

John Luke Tyner, a portfolio manager at Alabama-based Aptus Capital Advisors, said he's also thinking about how the lagged effects of the Fed's rate cuts could work their way through the economy - and end up turning into an inflation problem after this year. If the Fed also stops shrinking its large balance sheet by the end of this year, which would effectively end its quantitative-tightening program, this would translate into what could be the equivalent of yet another quarter-point rate cut, Tyner said.

"There are factors that could cause a reacceleration in inflation," such as more bank lending, increased demand for mortgages and refinancing, and the stimulative effect of President Trump's tax and spending law known as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, according to Tyner.

-Vivien Lou Chen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  10-24-25 1351ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Svante Honored with “Ambition” Award at BC Tech’s 2025 Technology Impact Awards
data.description:
Svante Honored with “Ambition” Award at BC Tech’s 2025 Technology Impact Awards

Svante Technologies Inc. (Svante), a global leader in solid sorbent-based carbon capture and removal solutions, is proud to announce that it has received the “Ambition” Award in the Company of the Year category at the BC Tech Association’s 2025 Technology Impact Awards (TIAs).

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251024685583/en/

Svante named "Company of the Year: Ambition" at the 2025 BC Tech Technology Impact Awards in Vancouver, BC, Canada.

The Ambition Award celebrates companies that demonstrate bold vision and relentless drive to scale their impact globally. Svante was recognized for its pioneering work in carbon capture and removal technology and its commitment to accelerating the commercial deployment of engineered solutions for carbon management worldwide.

Other finalists in this category included Building Bloc, PBC Solutions, and SenseNet.

“Winning the Ambition Award underscores our determination to lead the world in tackling emissions from the biogenic, industrial, and energy sectors,” said Claude Letourneau, President & CEO of Svante. “Our team is focused on scaling breakthrough carbon capture and removal solutions – and we made a bold bet to do just that by commissioning our 141,000 sq ft filter manufacturing facility, which is now able to make enough filters to capture and remove 10 million tonnes of CO2 annually. By bringing the supply to the market, we’re enabling industries to meet their decarbonization targets while also creating sustainable economic growth both in Canada and abroad.”

This award follows on several large milestones that Svante has reached this year, including the company’s grand opening event (GO25), which celebrated the commissioning of its Centre of Excellence for Carbon Capture & Removal, a facility that manufactures Svante’s solid sorbent-based filters. Further, the company was inducted into the Global Cleantech Group’s Cleantech 100 Hall of Fame and was named on TIME & Statista’s Top 100 Greentech Companies list. Mid-year, Svante announced new carbon capture and removal projects, and rolled out new business units, and welcomed new investments.

About Svante

Svante is a purpose-driven, leading carbon capture and removal solutions provider. The company makes nanoengineered filters and modular rotating contactor machines that capture and remove CO2 in an environmentally responsible way from industrial emissions and the air. Svante is on the 2025 Global Cleantech 100 and TIME & Statista’s list of Top Greentech Companies of 2025. For more information, visit www.svanteinc.com.

Svante
Colleen Nitta
Director, Marketing & Communications
cnitta@svanteinc.com
+1-604-970-2813

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251024685583/en/

data.title: Ugg and Hoka sales fall in the U.S. as Americans change the way they shop
data.description:
By Steve Gelsi

Deckers Outdoors warns of soft direct-to-consumer sales because shoppers are shifting to 'multibrand in-store shopping experiences'

Deckers expects to be navigating a more cautious consumer environment this holiday season.

Deckers Outdoor Corp.'s stock fell to a two-year low Friday after the maker of Ugg boots and Hoka running shoes provided a disappointing full-year outlook as sales in the U.S., its biggest market, continued to decline.

The problem the company (DECK) is having is that consumer sentiment is still pressured, and Americans have become more discerning in how they spend their money because of worries about the economy and the potentially inflationary impacts of tariffs. There has also been a change in shopper behavior, which has hurt direct-to-consumer business among the company's brands, which also include Teva.

As U.S. consumers deal with "a more challenging macroeconomic environment" and higher prices, there has been a shift in consumer preferences toward "multibrand, in-store shopping experiences," said Chief Financial Officer Steve Fasching, according to a FactSet transcript of a post-earnings-report conference call with analysts.

There is also a concern that shoppers will become even more discerning in the coming months, including over the holidays, which could hurt sales for the second half of the fiscal year as prices continue to rise.

MarketWatch Live: Consumers now foresee a 4.6% inflation rate over the coming year: University of Michigan survey

"We are anticipating a more cautious consumer as the full impact of tariffs and price increases will be felt here in the U.S.," Deckers Chief Executive Stefano Caroti said on the company's second-quarter conference call with analysts, according to a FactSet transcript.

The company, which didn't provided full-year sales guidance when it reported first-fiscal-quarter results in July, said Thursday in its second-quarter report that fiscal 2026 net sales were expected to be about $5.35 billion. That was below the $5.38 billion average analyst estimate compiled by FactSet.

The company also projects full-year earnings of $6.30 to $6.39 a share, against a FactSet consensus of $6.39 a share.

"As U.S. consumers are beginning to see some price increases, it is impacting their purchase behavior within the consumer discretionary space," said CFO Fasching.

The stock tumbled 12.8% by midday, enough to pace the S&P 500 index's SPX decliners on the day. It was also on track for its lowest close since Oct. 26, 2023.

Among the key numbers reported for the second fiscal quarter, which ended Sept. 30, Deckers said domestic net sales retreated by 1.7% from a year earlier to $839.5 million, while international net sales jumped 29.3% to $591.3 million.

And direct-to-consumer, or DTC, sales fell 0.8% to $394.6 million. Hoka DTC sales were up 8%, but that was driven by international growth, while Ugg DTC sales fell 10%.

The company's second-quarter profit increased to $277.13 million, or $1.82 a share, from $253.1 million, or $1.59 a share, in the year-ago quarter. The company handily beat the FactSet consensus earnings estimate of $1.58 a share.

Sales rose by 9.1% to $1.43 billion, just ahead of the analyst estimate, which pegged quarterly revenue at $1.42 billion.

Jefferies analyst Ashley Helgans said Deckers missed her second-quarter targets for Ugg sales and direct-to-consumer sales. "We view the direct-to-consumer slowdown as disappointing to investors given expectations for ... improvement," said Helgans.

Reduced Ugg sales momentum could force the company to cut prices, she said.

Including Friday's moves, Deckers shares have fallen 56% in 2025, while the benchmark S&P 500 SPX has risen 15.7%.

-Steve Gelsi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  10-24-25 1338ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Majority Grindr Shareholders George Raymond Zage III and James Fu Bin Lu Submit Non-Binding Offer to Acquire All Outstanding Shares of Grindr Inc. for $18.00 per Share
data.description:
Majority Grindr Shareholders George Raymond Zage III and James Fu Bin Lu Submit Non-Binding Offer to Acquire All Outstanding Shares of Grindr Inc. for $18.00 per Share

Majority Grindr Shareholders George Raymond Zage III and James Fu Bin Lu Submit Non-Binding Offer to Acquire All Outstanding Shares of Grindr Inc. for $18.00 per Share

PR Newswire

LOS ANGELES, Oct. 24, 2025

Proposed Offer Represents 51% Premium to Unaffected Stock Price

LOS ANGELES, Oct. 24, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- George Raymond Zage III and James Fu Bin Lu (together, the "Proposing Shareholders"), who along with affiliated entities beneficially own in aggregate more than 60% of the outstanding shares of common stock of Grindr Inc. (the "Company" or "Grindr") (NYSE: GRND), today submitted a non-binding proposal (the "Proposal") to acquire all outstanding shares of the Company's common stock not already owned by the Proposing Shareholders and their affiliates (the "Minority Shares") in a going-private transaction (the "Acquisition"). 

The Proposal contemplates consideration of $18.00 per share for each share of common stock, representing an approximately 51% premium over the unaffected stock price on October 10, 2025, the trading day immediately prior to the day the Proposing Shareholders first informed the Company of their intention to explore a going-private transaction. The Proposing Shareholders have secured significant expressions of interest to participate in financing, including multiple highly confident letters and contributions of equity, and are confident that these sources will be fully sufficient to fund the Acquisition. 

"We acquired Grindr in 2020 and have been intently focused on building a world class management team that is focused on improving the product for the community Grindr serves," said Zage. "We are strong believers in the long-term outlook for the Company—I have been a consistent buyer of shares in Grindr since listing, buying over $200m of shares on the public market and am also willing to contribute additional equity to this deal. Further, we have received considerable initial interest from both debt and equity investors in participating in this opportunity. We hope to have an active and friendly dialogue with our CEO George Arison and the board to find the best path forward for the Company, our employees and investors." 

"We are pleased to submit this Proposal, which represents a significant premium to recent trading prices and better positions the Company for focused growth as a private entity," said Lu.  "We look forward to engaging constructively with the Company and other shareholders in executing on our proposal."

Zage and Lu originally acquired Grindr in June 2020, and led the Company's public listing in November 2022.  They have each served on the Grindr Board of Directors since the acquisition in 2020, with Lu serving as Chairman during that time. 

Media Contacts 
Steve Bruce / Taylor Ingraham
ASC Advisors
sbruce@ascadvisors.com / tingraham@ascadvisors.com
203 992 1230

Disclaimer

This press release is not a solicitation of a proxy or vote with respect to any securities of the Company or any other securities, or an offer to purchase or a solicitation of an offer to sell any securities of the Company or any other securities, and it is not a substitute for any proxy statement or other filings that may be made with the Securities and Exchange Commission ("SEC") should this proposed transaction go forward. If such documents are filed with the SEC, investors will be urged to thoroughly review and consider them because they will contain important information, including risk factors. Any such documents, once filed, will be available free of charge at the SEC's website (www.sec.gov) and from the Company.

View original content:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/majority-grindr-shareholders-george-raymond-zage-iii-and-james-fu-bin-lu-submit-non-binding-offer-to-acquire-all-outstanding-shares-of-grindr-inc-for-18-00-per-share-302594153.html

SOURCE George Raymond Zage III & James F. Lu

data.title: What this week's gold-price decline might signal for your investment portfolio
data.description:
By Philip van Doorn

Also: A big week for oil, healthcare's doldrums and buying opportunities for investors, tech stocks and Social Security news

The price of gold has soared this year, but on Oct. 21, the action reversed with a sharp decline.

Let's begin with a two-year chart showing the movement of gold prices through early Friday:

Gold's relentless drive was reversed, at least momentarily, on Oct. 21, when the price of front-month contracts on the New York Mercantile Exchange declined 5.7% to $4,087.70 an ounce. Gold was still up 55.5% from the beginning of 2025 through then.

On Tuesday, front-month contract prices for gold (GC00) on the New York Mercantile Exchange declined 5.7% to $4,087.70 an ounce. This was the largest price drop for gold in more than 10 years.

Myra Saefong and Gordon Gottsegen spoke with professional money managers about what the broad action might mean for investors holding gold as a long-term investment.

More coverage digging deep into gold trading and investing trends:

-- After gold's big plunge, here's what history shows could happen next

-- Here's a theory about why gold suffered its biggest one-day fall in more than 10 years, and it's linked to the U.S. economy

-- Need to Know: Gold just stumbled. A JPMorgan strategist says the metal could double in value.

-- Gold prices are so high, even central banks are feeling FOMO

Oil's catalyst

For one week through Thursday, the S&P 500 SPX returned 1.7%. Here is how the 11 sectors of the U.S. large-cap benchmark index performed:

The energy sector - dominated by Exxon Mobil and Chevron - led the sectors of the S&P 500 this week.

All returns in this article include reinvested dividends.

The S&P 500 energy sector was the best performer for one week through Thursday, returning 4.3%. One factor behind the price increase was a new set of sanctions by the U.S. against Russian oil producers. Myra Saefong spoke with Phil Flynn, senior market analyst at the Price Futures Group, who described what might be a more important factor in the perking-up of crude oil prices.

One easy way to invest in the largest U.S. oil producers is by holding shares of the Energy Select SPDR ETF XLE. This exchange-traded fund holds all 23 stocks in the S&P 500's energy sector. Since it is weighted by market capitalization (as are the S&P 500 and its sectors), the ETF's portfolio is 42% concentrated in two companies: Exxon Mobil Corp. (XOM), and Chevron Corp. (CVX).

More: Buyers of Russian oil now risk playing a high-stakes poker game, as fresh sanctions on Moscow lift prices

Opportunities in the healthcare sector

The S&P 500 was up 15.8% for 2025 through Thursday, while the healthcare sector was up 7.6%. There have been several factors working against large-cap companies in the healthcare sector as a group this year, including shifts in federal policy under the One Big Beautiful Act, signed into law by President Donald Trump in July.

One reason for the current partial shutdown of the federal government is the pending expiration of boosts to subsidies for Affordable Care Act insurance plans that were put in place under the 2021 Inflation Reduction Act signed into law by President Joe Biden. Democratic leaders in Congress favor extending the additional subsidies, while the Republican leadership wants the subsidies to expire at the end of 2025.

If we look back five years through Thursday, the healthcare sector of the S&P 500 has been the second-worst performer among the 11 sectors, with a return of 47.4%. The worst-performing sector has been real estate, with a 42.3% return, while the full index has returned 109.5%, according to LSEG.

A stock screen among the S&P 500's three cheapest sectors (based on price/earnings ratios) highlighted 10 stocks of companies expected to show strong growth numbers through 2027, including three in the healthcare sector.

And Julien Albertini of First Eagle Investments in New York made the case for buying when a sector is out of favor while naming three of his favorite healthcare stocks selected for attractive valuation and growth prospects.

Social Security - enjoy a COLA

On Friday, the Social Security Administration announced that the cost-of-living adjustment for 2026 would be 2.8%. Jessica Hall explained how the 2026 COLA compares to those of previous years and how your Social Security benefits are taxed.

More on Social Security benefits and planning

-- Social Security could adopt a 'senior-friendly' COLA index - but retirees still won't get more money

-- How you can stretch your Social Security benefits

-- Help Me Retire: Does my sick husband have to reapply for spousal benefits when I get Social Security? He only gets $800 a month.

Thoughts about the federal shutdown

The partial shutdown of the federal government began on Oct. 1 It may stretch into November. The longest such shutdown was 35 days during Trump's first term.

Victor Reklaitis looked into the expected economic damage from the shutdown and what events might cause Democrats and the Trump administration to resume negotiations on a federal budget deal.

A technology stock screen

The S&P 500 is highly concentrated, with 10 companies comprising about 40% of its weighting. Five companies - Nvidia Corp. (NVDA), Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), Apple Inc. (AAPL), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (GOOG) and Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) - together make up 29.5% of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust SPY.

Christine Ji wrote about five bubble-resistant tech stocks that can lower your overall investment risk as you play the artificial intelligence trend.

Another screen: These low-risk stocks could be a profitable answer to this volatile earnings season

Possible signal of an overheated stock market: The race to launch ever-riskier leveraged ETFs in the U.S. is heating up

The Moneyist: 'A friend calls it the everything bubble': Why do so many economists fear a 1929-style crash?

If you have a house built, watch for this tax trap

Aarthi Swaminathan explained how people who own newly constructed homes can fall into a tax trap a year later.

More coverage of the housing market:

-- Home sellers have a 'tough pill to swallow' this fall, real-estate agents warn

-- Mortgage rates drop to the lowest level in a year, opening 'an important window' for buyers

-- This one number explains the growing gulf between people who can afford to buy a house today - and those who can't

Are you frustrated with repeated health insurance denials? There is hope.

As part of the Price of Protection series, Genna Contino explained how you can use artificial intelligence to improve your ability to appeal to your health insurer after your claim or pre-approval is denied.

How to spice up Six Flags - and its stock

Bill Peters explained how the involvement of Travis Kelce can help Six Flags broaden its appeal and increase theme-park attendance.

More from the MarketWatch Companies team:

-- Why Ford is doubling down on the F-150 - and redeploying EV workers

-- Why Molson Coors's new CEO needs more than job cuts to shore up the bottom line

-- Smaller is better for Honeywell, as profit outlook is raised ahead of Solstice spinoff

-- Why this regional bank's stock should rebound, after loan losses triggered an 'asymmetrical' selloff

Here's where President Donald Trump might want to invest more of your money

Australia's prime minister, Anthony Albanese, and U.S. President Donald Trump spoke to reporters after signing an $8.5 billion rare earth minerals agreement on Oct. 20. That deal followed Trump's decision to have the federal government invest in U.S. and Canadian rare-earth producers.

This year, the federal government has taken equity stakes in a bunch of companies, including Intel Corp (INTC)., MP Materials Corp. (MP), Lithium Americas Corp. (LAC) and others.

An obvious wonder for investors is where Trump might look next for more investments of taxpayers' money.

James Rogers and Victor Reklaitis covered the government's recent investments in rare-earth companies and named more industry players that might catch Trump's eye.

Looking further ahead at speculative investments, Emily Bary and Christine Ji considered the possibility of government investment in companies working to develop quantum computing technology.

More from the MarketWatch Technology team:

-- This underrated Tesla business deserves more attention - and it's not AI

-- Intel earnings aren't swaying the skeptics: 'This fight is far from over.'

-- Super Micro's latest financial update adds to credibility concerns

-- Google shows off its semiconductor prowess with a new Anthropic deal

Want more from MarketWatch? Sign up for this and other newsletters to get the latest news and advice on personal finance and investing.

-Philip van Doorn

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  10-24-25 1321ET

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 1 PM ET: Inflation Edges Up, But Less Than Expected, to 3% Rate | Consumer ...
data.description:
Inflation Edges Up, But Less Than Expected, to 3% Rate

The September data gives the Fed a clear path for widely expected rate cuts heading into their remaining meetings this year.

----

Consumer Sentiment Drops in October Michigan Survey

Stubborn inflation is weighing heavily on shoppers' mood, according to the University of Michigan's survey.

----

U.S. Stocks Rise After Inflation Report

Stocks rose as investors welcomed softer-than-expected inflation data.

----

Shutdown Puts October Inflation Report at Risk

The extended government shutdown has put collecting most October inflation data out of reach for the Bureau of Labor Statistics, according to people familiar with the agency's operations.

----

Trump Says He Is Terminating Trade Negotiations With Canada

The president criticized Ottawa for an advertisement featuring Ronald Reagan speaking negatively about tariffs.

----

U.S. And European Business Activity Pick Up in Continued Show Of Resilience

A rise in activity in the U.S., Europe and Asia indicates that the global economy remains resilient in the face of higher tariffs and greater uncertainty about relations between powerful countries.

----

Full Impact of Tariffs on Asia-Pacific Still to Come, IMF Warns

Asia-Pacific economies fared better than expected in the first half of the year but the IMF warns that the full blow of U.S. tariff hikes remains unclear, and growth will slow.

----

U.K. Retail Sales Rise for Fourth Straight Month

Retail sales volumes were unexpectedly higher in September, rising 0.5% on month, helped by tech stores and demand for gold.

----

Russia's Central Bank Cuts Key Rate as New Sanctions Loom

The cut comes as an already slowing economy braces for the impact of fresh sanctions in response to President Vladimir Putin's continued war on Ukraine.

----

Japan's Consumer Inflation Picks Up, But Rate Hike Timing Still Unclear

Consumer inflation-excluding volatile fresh food prices-increased 2.9% last month from a year earlier, compared with August's 2.7% rise.

----

Will India and China Be Able to Resist U.S. Sanctions on Russian Oil?

New Delhi and Beijing have so far resisted U.S. calls to stop buying Russian crude, but far-reaching sanctions will be hard to ignore.

----

Strong Earnings Reassure Jittery, Data-Deprived Investors

Corporate earnings are easing anxieties about the U.S. economy, providing support to markets buffeted by trade tensions with China and worries about bad business loans.

----

Gold Price Drops Again. What's Halting Its 9-Week Winning Run.

The precious metal is down 3.7% for the week so far but remains up 56% in 2025

----

China's New Strategy for Trump: Punch Hard, Concede Little

For Xi, rare-earths bombshell signaled a new toughness, while TikTok was 'spiritual opium' that could be turned into a low-cost bargaining chip.

----

BOJ Policy Should Align With Government Policy, Japan's Finance Minister Says

Japan's new finance minister said it is necessary for the administration and the BOJ to maintain close contact as monetary policy must be consistent with government policy. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 24, 2025 13:15 ET (17:15 GMT)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 1 PM ET: Porsche's Operating Profit Slumps as EV Challenges, China Woes Take Toll | Brookfield ...
data.description:
Porsche's Operating Profit Slumps as EV Challenges, China Woes Take Toll

Slow electric-vehicle rollout, weak demand for German premium cars in China and U.S. President Trump's tariffs have taken a toll on the sport-car maker.

----

Brookfield in Advanced Talks for Massive Nuclear Project in South Carolina

Nuclear energy is having a comeback as big tech's AI push stokes demand.

----

GM Cuts More Than 200 White-Collar Jobs in Detroit

The move comes days after GM's stock surged to a record following better-than-expected earnings and a rosier outlook.

----

Procter & Gamble to Focus on Innovation, Not Discounts, to Attract Wary Shoppers

Procter & Gamble's sales grew in the first quarter and the consumer products company expects a milder effect from tariffs this fiscal year.

----

Alaska Airlines Works to Restore Operations After Tech Outage Grounds Flights

The carrier postponed its quarterly earnings call after the hourslong ground stop Thursday night.

----

Booz Allen Cuts Jobs, Fiscal-Year Outlook as Government Funding Stalls

Booz Allen slashed its outlook for the year and trimmed its workforce further as it faces a prolonged and severe slowdown in government-contract funding, particularly in its civil sector.

----

Big Banks Enter Stablecoin Era by Taking Zelle International

The move could help the big bank owners of the payment product head off cryptocurrency competition.

----

General Dynamics Revenue Rises on Stronger Business-Jet Demand

General Dynamics posted higher third-quarter revenue and profit, driven by increased orders for business jets as corporate profits continue to grow and a new tax law offered companies incentives to buy planes.

----

Meta Charged by EU Over Handling of Illegal Content

The European Commission said the Facebook owner fails to give users a simple way to flag such content.

----

Beyond Meat Expects Impairment Charge, Revenue In Line With Target

The plant-based meat producer plans to book a charge in the latest quarter related to certain assets, though revenue for the period is set to be in line with the company's target.

----

HCA Lifts Outlook as Quarterly Profit, Revenue Rise

HCA Healthcare lifted its full-year outlook after logging higher profit and revenue in the third quarter, as same-facility admissions grew.

----

Investors Love Intel Again. That Still Doesn't Solve Its Problems.

Despite a rally in the storied chip maker's stock price, there is little evidence of a real turn in the company's fortunes.

----

Sanofi Earnings Get Boost From Dupixent, New Drugs as U.S. Talks Continue

Shares rose after a jump in sales of the blockbuster Dupixent drug and robust pharmaceutical launches delivered higher revenue and adjusted earnings.

----

UBS Names New Chief Operating Officer in Executive Reshuffle

Lukas Gaehwiler, vice chairman of UBS's board since 2022, will retire following next year's annual general meeting after a 45-year career in the sector.

----

Safran Raises Outlook, Forecasts Residual Tariff Hit

The aerospace-industry supplier's new 2025 outlook includes the impact of tariffs, which the company had excluded up until now due to uncertainty about their effect.

----

Lithium Giant Cheers U.S., Australia Minerals Pact, Warns on Price Supports

The chief executive of Pilbara Minerals praised a new critical minerals deal between the U.S. and Australia as a fillip to building out global supply chains for commodities essential to modern technologies. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  October 24, 2025 13:15 ET (17:15 GMT)
